dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I'm having trouble breathing. I woke up and found it hard to get air about an hour ago.
Can you describe any other symptoms you're experiencing?
I'm working very hard to breathe, with grunting when I exhale, and I feel short of breath.
Can you tell me your name?
My name is James.
Where are we right now?
We are in Richmond.
What is the date today?
I'm not sure, maybe the 24th?
Why did you call 911?
Because I can't breathe.
Your airway is patent and you're maintaining it on your own. Can you speak in full sentences?
Yes, I can.
Your breathing seems rapid. Do you use muscles like your neck or shoulders to breathe?
Yes, I feel like I'm working hard to breathe.
You're breathing about 24 times per minute. Any chest pain?
I don't feel chest pain, but I am feeling very short of breath.
Your pulse is strong and your radial pulse is present, and your skin is warm. Do you have a history of breathing issues?
No, but I do have kidney issues and diabetes.
Pulse is 104.
Respirations are 24.
Blood pressure is 226 over 106.
SpO2 is 96%.
EKG is sinus tachycardia.
No arrhythmias noted on monitor.
When did this trouble begin, and what were you doing when it started?
It began when I was in bed trying to sleep about an hour ago. I was lying flat, and I suddenly struggled to inhale.

dialogue.role: events leading up to the event; EMT
dialogue.utterance: Has anything like this happened before?

dialogue.role: events leading up to the event; Patient
dialogue.utterance: Three days ago, at the hospital, I had similar symptoms and they gave me CPAP and furosemide.
What are your known medical conditions?
I have end-stage kidney disease, hypertension, and am insulin-dependent for diabetes. I require regular dialysis.
Do you have any known allergies?
I am allergic to Sulfa and Penicillin.
What medications are you currently taking?
I take Hydralazine.
May I confirm your age and gender?
I'm 42 years old and male.
On a scale from 0 to 10, 10 being the worst shortness of breath you've had, how would you rate it?
I would say it's a 10. I can't breathe at all right now.
We'll continue to monitor this and reassess.
You are alert and oriented to person, place, time, situation (AOx4).
We don't note any bleeding. Your skin color appears normal.
You walked over to the stretcher. Can you move your arms and legs independently?
Yes, I can move my arms and legs independently.
Based on your breathing difficulties and your medical conditions, I'll proceed with the pulmonary edema and CHF protocol.
We're going to place you on CPAP. You don't have any contraindications for it.
cpap application; EMT: Can we put the CPAP device on to help you breathe more easily?
cpap application; Patient: If it helps, yes.
monitoring; EMT: You'll also be placed on a cardiac monitor to detect any heart rhythm changes.
I have placed you on a 4-lead ECG monitor.
Pulse is now 97. 54. Take Vital Signs; retake vital signs: resp; Partner: Respirations have increased to 31. 55. Take Vital Signs; retake vital signs: bp; Partner: Blood pressure is 227 over 128.
SpO2 has risen to 100%.
Glucose is 176.
Your GCS is 15 (E4, V5, M6) - you're alert and oriented.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how would you rate your shortness of breath now?
It's better, maybe an 8. 61. Take Vital Signs; retake vital signs: pulse; Partner: Pulse is 98. 62. Take Vital Signs; retake vital signs: resp; Partner: Respirations have decreased to 18.
Blood pressure is 225 over 134. 64. Take Vital Signs; retake vital signs: spo2; Partner: SpO2 is at 99%.
You walked a couple of steps from your front door to the stretcher. Do you have a steady gait?
Yes, my gait is steady.
EKG is showing a sinus rhythm.
No pitting edema noted in your lower legs.
I can hear coarse crackles or rales in all lung fields.
Glucose is not obtainable at this time.
We've applied 1 inch of nitro paste on your left upper chest for transdermal administration.
Furosemide 4 milligrams IVP administered after a two-provider cross-check.
We've placed an 18G IV catheter in the antecubital vein of your right arm.
A saline lock has been attached to the IV site to allow for medication administration.
Pulse is stable at 98. 76. Take Vital Signs; retake vital signs: resp; Partner: Retaken respirations are 24.

Blood pressure is now 234 over 123. 78. Take Vital Signs; retake vital signs: glucose; Partner: No blood sugar available at this time. 79. Take Vital Signs; retake vital signs: spo2; Partner: SpO2 is up to 100%. 80. Take Vital Signs; retake vital signs: spo2; Partner: SpO2 has decreased slightly to 98%. 81. Take Vital Signs; retake vital signs: bp; Partner: Blood pressure is 227 over 117.
EKG remains in sinus rhythm.
We have placed an IV in the antecubital vein of your right arm for medication access.
Patient has been placed in a position of comfort, covered with two blankets, and secured with five-point restraints. He is now loaded onto the ambulance.
The patient has moved independently from the stretcher to the ED bed upon arrival.
We've loaded the patient and are en route to VCU Health Systems.
Vital signs and interventions have remained stable during transport.
The patient is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
Last SpO2 is 100%, and vital signs are flat. A 12-lead ECG was repeated en route with findings consistent for a male over 40 - no STEMI noted.
